questions.question: Explain the representation of a stack using arrays with necessary C language declarations. Also explain the significance of the 'top' variable.
questions.hint: Remember to initialize top to -1 to indicate an empty stack
keyPoints: Define MAX constant for maximum stack size
Declare array to store stack elements
Initialize top variable to -1 (indicates empty stack)
top tracks the index of topmost element

questions.question: Write a C function to implement the PUSH operation for a stack implemented using an array. Also explain the overflow condition.
questions.hint: Always check for overflow before pushing
keyPoints: Check if stack is full (top == MAX-1)
If full, display overflow message or return error
Overflow occurs when top reaches MAX-1

questions.question: Write a C function to implement the POP operation for a stack implemented using an array. Also explain the underflow condition.
questions.hint: Always check for underflow before popping
keyPoints: If empty, display underflow message or return error
If not empty, store stack[top] in variable

questions.question: Explain the PEEK (or TOP) operation in stack implementation using arrays. Write a C function for the same.
questions.hint: PEEK is similar to POP but doesn't change top
keyPoints: PEEK returns top element without removing it
Check if stack is empty first (top == -1)
Does not modify the top variable
Used to view top element before popping

questions.question: Differentiate between Overflow and Underflow conditions in stack implementation. How are these conditions checked in array implementation?
questions.hint: Overflow happens on push attempt to full stack; Underflow happens on pop attempt from empty stack
keyPoints: Overflow: Stack is full, cannot insert more elements
Underflow: Stack is empty, cannot pop elements

questions.question: Write C functions to check whether the stack is empty and whether the stack is full. Explain their significance.
questions.hint: These functions help avoid overflow and underflow errors
keyPoints: isFull(): returns true if top == MAX-1
Used as precondition checks before push/pop operations
Return type should be boolean or int

questions.question: Write a complete C program to implement a stack using arrays with operations: initialize, push, pop, peek, and display. Include necessary error handling for overflow and underflow.
questions.hint: Structure the program with separate functions for each operation
keyPoints: Declare global stack array and top variable
Implement peek() to return top element
Implement display() to show all elements

questions.question: Explain the algorithm for PUSH and POP operations in array implementation of stack. Also analyze the time complexity of these operations.
questions.hint: Array implementation provides O(1) for all stack operations
keyPoints: PUSH Algorithm: Check overflow, increment top, assign value
POP Algorithm: Check underflow, get value, decrement top
All operations are constant time because array provides direct access

questions.question: Compare stack implementation using arrays with stack implementation using linked lists. Discuss advantages and disadvantages of each.
questions.hint: Consider memory allocation, size limitations, and performance
keyPoints: Array: Fixed size, Linked List: Dynamic size
Array: Memory pre-allocated, Linked List: Memory allocated as needed
Array: O(1) push/pop, Linked List: O(1) push/pop
Array: May have unused memory or overflow
Linked List: No overflow (until heap memory exhausts)
Linked List: Requires extra memory for pointers
Array: Faster (no malloc/free), Linked List: Slower

questions.question: Design a stack to evaluate postfix expressions. Explain how the stack is used and trace the evaluation of the expression: 6 2 + 3 * 8 4 / -
questions.hint: Remember: for operator, pop second operand first for left operand
keyPoints: Postfix: operands pushed, operators pop two operands

questions.question: Explain how a stack is used for function call management in programming languages. How is this related to array implementation?
questions.hint: The call stack is an example of stack implementation in OS/runtime
keyPoints: Function calls use stack data structure
Each function call creates an activation record (stack frame)
Stack frame contains: return address, local variables, parameters
PUSH called when function is invoked
Stack overflow can occur with deep recursion

questions.question: Consider a stack implemented using an array with MAX = 5. Initially top = -1. Perform the following operations sequentially and show the state of stack after each operation: PUSH(10), PUSH(20), PUSH(30), POP(), PUSH(40), PUSH(50), PUSH(60), POP(), PEEK()
questions.hint: Track the value of 'top' after each operation

questions.question: A stack is implemented using an array of size 100. If the current value of 'top' is 49, answer the following: (a) How many elements are in the stack? (b) What is the index of the bottom element? (c) Can we push 50 more elements? (d) What will be the value of 'top' after 51 more pop operations?
questions.hint: Remember: number of elements = top + 1, and minimum top can be -1
keyPoints: (c) No, only 50 more can be pushed (100-50=50)
(d) After 51 pops: top = 49 - 51 = -2, but stack becomes empty at top=-1, so underflow occurs after 50th pop
Actually: 49 - 51 = -2, which is impossible, so after 50 pops top=-1 (empty), 51st pop causes underflow

questions.question: Show step-by-step stack contents when checking balanced parentheses for the expression: ( ( A + B ) * ( C - D ) ) using array implementation. Explain the algorithm.
questions.hint: Only parentheses (opening and closing) are pushed/popped
keyPoints: Algorithm: Push opening brackets, pop for closing brackets
If stack not empty at end = unbalanced

questions.question: Trace the contents of a stack (array implementation) while converting the infix expression A + B * C to postfix using the Shunting Yard algorithm. Show the operator stack and output at each step.
keyPoints: Initialize empty operator stack and output
Read *: * has higher precedence than +, push *
End of input: Pop all operators from stack
